narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in competitive fields like this, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students—things like their early college grades, later school performance, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPA, family income, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it estimated a 43.2% probability of failing the bar exam, which is high enough that the school uses it as a threshold to deny admission, aiming to select applicants most likely to succeed. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was close to the typical student's 3.21 and stood out as your strongest factor, actually working in your favor by suggesting you're capable of solid academic work that aligns with bar passers. However, your lower decile ranking in Year 3 grades—below the average—pushed the prediction toward failure more than anything else, indicating the model saw this as a sign of potential struggles in advanced coursework. Your LSAT score of 25.50, well below the usual 36.70, also contributed toward the failure prediction, as higher scores are strongly linked to bar success in the data. Planning full-time studies helped nudge things positively, matching what most successful students do, and your Year 1 decile of 3 was slightly below average but still offered a small boost. Your middle family income bracket played a minor positive role too, similar to many who pass. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile is solid in GPA and study plans but lags in LSAT and later grades, which tipped the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential—models like this miss the full story of resilience many Black women bring to law. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year performance through targeted prep, and reapplying next cycle with updated transcripts; many overcome similar hurdles and succeed. You've got this—keep pushing.
